title: Los artículos y el género
description: Spanish articles (el/la/los/las, un/una/unos/unas) and the -o/-a gender rule.

cards.notes: Used before masculine singular nouns: *el libro*, *el chico*, *el perro*.
Used before feminine singular nouns: *la casa*, *la chica*, *la mesa*. Spanish doesn't elide before vowels the way French does — *la amiga*, not *l'amiga*.
Used for masculine or mixed-gender plurals: *los libros*, *los chicos* (boys, or boys+girls). One man in a group of women makes the group *los*.
Only for all-feminine plurals: *las casas*, *las chicas*.
Indefinite m. sg.: *un libro* (a book). Don't confuse with the number *uno* (one) — *un* is the shortened article form before a noun.
Approximate plural — *unos libros* (some books, around several). Optional in most plurals; *libros* alone often suffices.
Ends in **-e** — gender unpredictable from the ending. Memorise as masculine. (Latin American Spanish often uses *carro* — also masculine.)
**Common exception**: ends in -o but is feminine. *la mano*, *las manos*. There's a small set of these (also *la foto*, *la radio*) — memorise as exceptions.
**Common exception**: ends in -a but is masculine. *el día*, *los días*. Pair with *el mapa* (m.), *el problema* (m.) — Greek-origin words ending in -a are typically masculine.

steps.title: Gender in Spanish
steps.body:
# El género

Like French, Spanish nouns have **gender**: every noun is either masculine or feminine, and the articles + adjectives that go with it must match. Unlike French, Spanish gives you a reliable rule for most nouns:

- Nouns ending in **-o** are usually **masculine**: *libro*, *chico*, *coche*
- Nouns ending in **-a** are usually **feminine**: *casa*, *chica*, *mesa*

This is the single biggest grammar win Spanish has over French — you can guess the gender of ~85% of nouns just from the last letter.

The exceptions exist (we'll cover them) but they're a small, learnable list. For now, train your reflex: see -o → say *el*. See -a → say *la*.

steps.title: Definite articles — el, la, los, las
steps.body:
## The four forms of "the"

| | masculine | feminine |
|---|---|---|
| singular | **el** | **la** |
| plural | **los** | **las** |

- *el libro* — the book (m. sg.)
- *la casa* — the house (f. sg.)
- *los libros* — the books (m. pl.)
- *las casas* — the houses (f. pl.)

**Key difference from French**: Spanish DOESN'T elide before vowels. *la amiga* (the friend, f.), NOT *l'amiga*. Spanish keeps the article visible — easier to read out loud.

Mixed-gender plurals use **los**: *los chicos* covers a group of boys + girls. One man in a group of 20 women is still grammatically *los amigos*. (Modern Spanish has informal alternatives like *les amigues* but they're not at A1 level.)

exercise.prompt: Match each noun with its definite article.
Type the correct definite article for **libro** (book).

steps.title: Indefinite articles — un, una, unos, unas
steps.body:
## The four forms of "a / some"

| | masculine | feminine |
|---|---|---|
| singular | **un** | **una** |
| plural | **unos** | **unas** |

- *un libro* — a book
- *una casa* — a house
- *unos libros* — some books (a few)
- *unas casas* — some houses (a few)

**Tip on the plurals**: in Spanish, *unos / unas* are OPTIONAL in many sentences. *Tengo libros* (I have books) is more idiomatic than *Tengo unos libros* (I have some books). Use *unos / unas* when you specifically want to say *some* (around several, not many).

Difference from French: Spanish DOESN'T have a mandatory plural indefinite the way French does with *des*. French *J'ai des amigos* (mandatory); Spanish *Tengo amigos* (no article needed).

exercise.prompt: Pick the correct indefinite article for **casa** (house).

steps.title: Exceptions to memorise
steps.body:
## The -o/-a rule isn't universal

A small set of nouns breaks the pattern. The two most important at A1:

**Nouns ending in -a that are masculine** (often Greek origin):
- ***el día*** — the day
- ***el mapa*** — the map
- ***el problema*** — the problem
- ***el tema*** — the topic / theme

**Nouns ending in -o that are feminine**:
- ***la mano*** — the hand
- ***la foto*** — the photo (short for *fotografía*)
- ***la moto*** — the motorbike (short for *motocicleta*)
- ***la radio*** — the radio

**Nouns ending in -e** (gender unpredictable):
- *el coche* — the car (m.)
- *el café* — the coffee (m.)
- *la noche* — the night (f.)
- *la clase* — the class (f.)

For -e nouns, you have to learn the gender along with the noun. There's no shortcut.

exercise.prompt: Type the definite article for **mano** (hand). Watch out — this is an exception.
exercise.hint: Ends in -o but is feminine. *La mano*.

exercise.prompt: Type the definite article for **día** (day). Another exception.
exercise.hint: Ends in -a but is masculine. *El día*.

exercise.prompt: Match each noun with its definite plural article (los or las).

steps.title: Build: The book and the house

exercise.prompt: Arrange to mean *The book and the house*.

exercise.hint: *Las* is feminine plural. *Manos* is *hands* (feminine exception, ends in -o).
